figures.caption: Figure 12: Effect of budget set granularity (DeepSeek-V3 ×MATH). We compare Coarse {1, 8, 16}, Default {1, 2, 4, 8, 16}, and Fine {1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 16}. (a) Pareto frontiers: solid = learned GBM, dashed = Oracle upper bound; (b) Oracle label distribution at B = 2.0: Coarse is forced into a bimodal b = 1/b = 8 regime while Default and Fine spread mass across b ∈ {2, 4, 8} where the marginal accuracy gain is concentrated; (c) Marginal return: oracle task accuracy at B = 2.0 plotted against the number of budget tiers |B|, with the pp-gain from each additional granularity annotated on the connecting segments. The Coarse→Default step yields a large gain, while Default→Fine is essentially saturated.

Figure 13: Feature diagnostics for AdaCompute-GBM (B = 2.0, DeepSeek-V3 ×MATH). (a) GBM feature importance over all 16 features, sorted and colour-graded. (b) Per-oracle-class mean feature values, z-scored along each row so that red/blue cells highlight features that push a question toward or away from each budget class. Dark-red cells at b = 16 for has_fractions, has_square_roots, and has_equation show that these structural signals correspond to questions that genuinely benefit from high budgets, while single_pass_acc and avg_word_length split cleanly across the b = 1 vs. b ∈ {8, 16} axis.

Figure 8: Binary search diagnostics on DeepSeek-V3 × MATH for three target budgets B ∈ {1.5, 2.0, 3.0}. (a) λ-bracket trajectories (symlog axis); (b) cost bracket converging to each target (shaded region = slack, horizontal lines = target); (c) residual cost error |C̄(λmid) − B| on log scale, with the 0.05 tolerance line; (d) visited (λ, C̄) points overlaid on the monotone staircase C̄(λ) guaranteed by Theorem 1. Convergence is effectively complete within 10 iterations for every target.
Table 1: Main results: task accuracy and realized cost under budget constraints B ∈ {1.5, 2.0, 3.0}, mean±std over 3 seeds. Bold: best non-oracle result per column.

Figure 9: Lagrangian mechanism at three scales on DeepSeek-V3 ×MATH. (a) Per-question oracle budget trajectories b⋆(x;λ) for all 200 questions (coloured by single-pass accuracy, red curve = one highlighted responsive question, triangles mark its regime transitions); (b) Stacked area of population-level oracle label distribution as λ increases — the budget mass “drains” from b = 16 to b = 1; (c) Lagrangian scores fλ(x, b) for the highlighted question at three λ values, with triangles marking the argmax at each price. Collectively, the three panels illustrate how a single scalar λ prices compute globally while producing heterogeneous per-question decisions.
Table 5: Oracle gap and imitation accuracy by model and budget. Oracle Gap = Oracle Acc − GBM Acc.

Figure 10: Classifier architecture ablation (pooled: 3 MATH models × B ∈ {1.5, 2.0, 3.0}, 10 random 80/20 splits). (a) Task accuracy broken out per backbone: GBM and Random Forest dominate every model uniformly. (b) Empirical verification of Theorem 2. Each marker is one split × classifier, plotted at (imitation error ε, task regret Acc(π⋆) − Acc(π̂)); the dashed line is the theoretical upper bound M̄emp · ε using the average worst-case gap, and the dotted line is the loose supx Mλ(x) · ε bound. All empirical points lie well below the bound, and the slope of the empirical cloud matches M̄emp, confirming the predicted imitation→ regret reduction is tight up to a constant. (c) Row-normalised confusion matrix of the GBM (oracle row vs. predicted column). The mass concentrates on the diagonal and immediately adjacent cells, confirming that most errors are “near-misses” to neighbouring budgets — exactly the regime in which the Mλ-weighted bound in Theorem 2 predicts minimal task-level impact.

Figure 4: Compute–accuracy Pareto frontiers across four model–dataset pairs. Our method (blue) closely tracks the Oracle upper bound (green dashed) and consistently dominates Fixed-b (grey), Random (red), and Heuristic (orange) at every budget level. The largest relative gains occur at intermediate budgets (B = 2–6) where routing flexibility matters most.

Figure 6: Oracle allocation on DeepSeek-V3 × MATH. Top row: per-question scatter of oracle budget b⋆(x) against single-pass accuracy Acc(x, 1) (marker size ∝ maximum achievable accuracy gain across budgets); the red line shows the decile mean. Bottom row: stacked composition of oracle labels per difficulty quintile. As B increases, budget is redistributed from b = 1 toward b ∈ {4, 8, 16} primarily within the middle quintiles, empirically verifying the “inverted-U” allocation pattern.

Figure 7: Empirical verification of the four archetypes from Figure 1. (a) Mean accuracy curves of four K-means clusters (thin background = sample of individual questions); cluster counts in the legend. (b) Archetype composition per model–dataset: GSM8K is dominated by “Easy” (> 90%), while the three MATH settings show comparable mixtures. (c) Oracle budget assignment per archetype at B = 2.0 (DeepSeek-V3 ×MATH): the “Responsive” cluster absorbs the overwhelming majority of the b ∈ {4, 8, 16} budget, while “Easy” and “Hard” clusters are routed almost entirely to b = 1.
Table 4: Oracle label distribution: fraction of questions assigned each budget level.

Figure 11: Data efficiency across all four model–dataset combinations. Each panel shows task accuracy vs. training data fraction at three target budgets (B ∈ {1.5, 2.0, 3.0}, error bands over 4 random seeds). Dashed horizontal lines = oracle upper bound per B; dotted horizontal lines = Fixed-b baseline. The GBM crosses the Fixed-b baseline with only 20–25% of the training data on every setting and climbs monotonically toward the oracle upper bound, with diminishing returns beyond 60%.

Figure 5: Budget allocation heatmap: fraction of questions at each (difficulty, budget) cell for oracle labels (top) and learned GBM labels (bottom) at B ∈ {1.2, 1.5, 3.0} (DeepSeek-V3 ×MATH). The learned policy faithfully replicates the oracle’s difficulty-routing structure.
Table 6: Binary search results: target budget, found λ∗, achieved C̄, and absolute error.

Figure 3: Overview of the SOLVE-THEN-LEARN pipeline. Solve stage (offline): compute the Acc(x, b) matrix via API calls, then derive oracle labels via Lagrangian optimisation with binary search over λ. Learn stage (offline): train a lightweight GBM classifier on oracle labels using cheap text features. Deploy stage (online): predict budget for new questions at inference time. The entire training overhead is <1s; only the one-time API data collection is expensive.
Table 3: Per-difficulty-level accuracy at b = 1 and b = 16, and self-consistency (SC) gain.
